coredata.dc:description: Toluene, a harmful gas, is extremely harmful to living organisms, and its distribution and concentration in the environment have always attracted much attention. However, traditional detection methods have poor selectivity, cannot detect low concentrations, and the equipment is bulky, expensive, and time-consuming. In this study, a fluorescence spectroscopy method based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite cellulose membrane (CQDs@CMC membrane) was developed, which addressed the limitations of CQDs, such as excessive operational expenses, intricate detection procedures, and prolonged analysis duration, enabling highly sensitive and specifically targeted detection of toluene gas. Firstly, hydrophobic propylene glycol/betaine (Gly/Bet) CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal method, and Gly/Bet CQDs composite membranes were prepared by combining them with CMC membrane, and they were characterized separately. It was found that Gly/Bet CQDs had a slight blue shift compared to the optimal excitation wavelength of the Gly/Bet CQDs @CMC membrane. Gly/Bet CQDs were uniformly loaded on the CMC membrane, and the fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) increased to 5.3 ns and 18.34 ns, respectively. The detection of harmful gas toluene was carried out by Gly/Bet CQDs composite membrane, and the toluene concentration had a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane, and when the toluene concentration was in the range of 200–1400 ppm, the fluorescence intensity was in proportion to the toluene concentration. The detection limit for p-toluene is 0.452 ppm, which is very sensitive. According to the results, the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is capable of effectively detecting toluene gas, which offers a solid theoretical foundation for the later use of toluene emission in real industry and toluene gas detection in daily life. In addition, it detects toluene and interfering gases with high selectivity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Toluene, a harmful gas, is extremely harmful to living organisms, and its distribution and concentration in the environment have always attracted much attention. However, traditional detection methods have poor selectivity, cannot detect low concentrations, and the equipment is bulky, expensive, and time-consuming. In this study, a fluorescence spectroscopy method based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite cellulose membrane (CQDs@CMC membrane) was developed, which addressed the limitations of CQDs, such as excessive operational expenses, intricate detection procedures, and prolonged analysis duration, enabling highly sensitive and specifically targeted detection of toluene gas. Firstly, hydrophobic propylene glycol/betaine (Gly/Bet) CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal method, and Gly/Bet CQDs composite membranes were prepared by combining them with CMC membrane, and they were characterized separately. It was found that Gly/Bet CQDs had a slight blue shift compared to the optimal excitation wavelength of the Gly/Bet CQDs @CMC membrane. Gly/Bet CQDs were uniformly loaded on the CMC membrane, and the fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) increased to 5.3 ns and 18.34 ns, respectively. The detection of harmful gas toluene was carried out by Gly/Bet CQDs composite membrane, and the toluene concentration had a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane, and when the toluene concentration was in the range of 200–1400 ppm, the fluorescence intensity was in proportion to the toluene concentration. The detection limit for p-toluene is 0.452 ppm, which is very sensitive. According to the results, the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is capable of effectively detecting toluene gas, which offers a solid theoretical foundation for the later use of toluene emission in real industry and toluene gas detection in daily life. In addition, it detects toluene and interfering gases with high selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advancement of industrialization, especially in the chemical and mining industries, this has led to increasing global air pollution. Some companies emit VOC during the production of food, electricity and other necessities of life. VOCs mainly include benzene, toluene, formaldehyde, acetone [1–4]. Toluene is a class of organics that has a high vapor pressure and is volatile at room temperature [5,6]. Inhalation of toluene vapor in large quantities can cause dizziness, drowsiness and respiratory discomfort in humans [7–10]. The threshold of irritation for human eyes and nose when inhaling toluene vapor is 53.07 ppm, with a minimum of 265.37 ppm, which causes dizziness [11]. In March 2025, an acute toluene poisoning incident occurred in a sewage treatment plant in Shanghai, China. The air in the digestion tank exceeded the national health standard by 46 times. In August 2003, an acute toluene poisoning accident occurred in a chemical enterprise in Shandong Province, China. The results of on-site sampling showed that the concentration in the environment exceeded the national standard, resulting in liver damage in one patient. Considering the harmful effects of toluene on the environment, its detection in the urban atmosphere has attracted the interest of the scientific community.

Currently, conventional detection methods for toluene, such as meteorological chromatography, ion chromatography and chemiluminescence [12,13]. These methods are dependent on large instruments, apparatus, instruments, and the test steps are cumbersome and time-consuming, which makes it difficult to be used for rapid on-site detection. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a low-cost, highly stable, highly sensitive, portable and visual analysis method. Fluorescence spectrophotometry, as an efficient spectroscopic detection technique, is mainly based on the interaction between fluorescent probes and molecules to be measured, and the quantitative analysis of sample components is achieved by the fluorescence spectra produced by fluorescent substances under the irradiation of the excitation light source, which is characterized by quick response speed, elevated sensitivity and distinct specificity, and it has a wide range of applications in chemical analysis and detection [14].

In recent years fluorescent carbon nanomaterials have been widely used in many fields [15,16]. Especially, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantages of excellent fluorescence performance, high stability, and simple preparation. It has received extensive attention from researchers, and is widely used in fluorescent probes, biosensing, and other fields [17,18]. Ayad et al. [19] synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through the hydrothermal treatment of bay leaves. These CQDs was then combined with a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) electrode to create a sensor designed for detecting formaldehyde vapor in air at room temperature. The developed sensor demonstrated rapid response, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity toward formaldehyde, along with strong reproducibility. Tachapermpon et al. [20] developed N-doped CQDs with high selectivity for toxic formaldehyde gas. When exposed to formaldehyde, the N-doped CQDs on the surface of Fusarium albicans leaves exhibited a visible fluorescence quenching signal at a concentration of 50.0 μg/L. This detection level is significantly lower than the World Health Organization (WHO) guideline for the maximum exposure limit of indoor air pollutants. Maruthapandi et al. [21] developed polymer-loaded fluorescent carbon dots (P-CQDs) by reacting tetraaminobenzene with 2,4,6-trichlorophenyl oxalic acid. The composite material has high stability, and its fluorescence is sharply quenched by several VOC analyzers (ethanolamine, diethanolamine, triethanolamine, ammonia), showing excellent performance when used as a VOC sensor.

However, traditional CQDs composites are expensive to prepare and complex to operate. Extensive research has demonstrated that incorporating nanomaterials into membranes can significantly enhance their performance, particularly in terms of permeability and selectivity. Typically, gas-separation polymer membranes embedded with nanoparticles can be modified through physical doping methods—such as dispersion, polarity adjustment, and hydrogen bonding—as well as chemical bonding techniques. These approaches enable the development of membranes with superior performance characteristics [22–24]. Cellulose is the world's most abundant green resource, containing a large number of hydroxyl structures, and has the characteristics of softness, transparency, and luminescence [25,26]. Cellulose also has excellent membranes-forming properties, and cellulose membranes can reduce problems such as carbon dot aggregation alone, fluorescence instability due to aggregation [27], and gas adsorption [28]. Junka et al. [29] prepared fluorescent nanopaper using CQDs to modify nanocellulose. By observing the surface and cross-sectional morphology of the membrane before and after modification, it was found that the addition of CQDs led to an increase in the roughness and pore structure of the membrane. The fluorescent nanomaterial maintains the good fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots, increasing the potential of cellulose membranes in anti-counterfeiting and biosensing applications. Shi et al. [30] prepared two kinds of CQDs，polymer-like QDs (PQD) and graphene oxide QDs (GQD) into Pebax, a poly(ether-b-amide) copolymer. By comparison, the modified GQD has better selectivity to CO2, so the gas separation performance of the membrane is obviously enhanced, and the performance of GQD is better than that of PQD. The membrane with 0.5 wt% GQD loading shows the best gas separation performance. Wang et al. [31] prepared N-CQDs@In2O3 ordered porous membranes on the surface of optical fibers by template method, and realized the detection of acetone gas. It has the advantages of low cost, good preparation repeatability, and low detection concentration of acetone gas. Therefore, the modified cellulose membranes can not only maintain the good optical properties of CQDs, but also increase the surface diffusion of gases.

In this study, a high-efficiency fluorescence method for the detection of harmful gases in complex and multi-interference environments was established. This method has the advantages of visualization, environmental friendliness, high selectivity and high sensitivity. When betaine (Bet) interacts with CO groups, it acts as an electron donor, forming stable electron pairs with the electron-withdrawing groups on the benzene ring. Glycerol (Gly) reacts with Bet to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) containing nitrogen-functional groups, which can form hydrogen bonds with toluene. This interaction enhances the adsorption of toluene gas by the CQDs composite, enabling efficient detection of toluene gas. In this study, a deep eutectic solvent (DES) composed of Gly and Bet was prepared as the precursor for CQDs, with Gly serving as the hydrogen bond donor and Bet as the hydrogen bond acceptor. Hydrophobic Gly/Bet CQDs was synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. These CQDs was then combined with carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) through hydrophobic modification to prepare the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC composite membrane. Through a series of characterization methods, the physicochemical properties of Gly/Bet CQDs and Gly Bet CQDs@CMC membrane and the detection effect of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane on toluene gas were investigated. Research shows that，the efficient detection of CQDs@CMC membrane is expected to be used for the rapid and visual detection of organic harmful gases.

Betaine (Bet) was purchased from Runyou Chemical Co., Ltd., glycerol (Gly) was purchased from Wuxi Yatai United Chemical Co., Ltd., ethanol, toluene and xylene were all from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd., anhydrous ethanol and carboxymethyl cellulose (C8H16O8) were all from Xilong Science and Technology Co., Ltd., (the DS of carboxymethyl cellulose is 0.4–1.0)， methanol was from Shanghai McLin Biochemical Co., Ltd., and benzene was from Tianjin Komio Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. All materials and reagents were of analytical purity grade and were utilized directly without undergoing additional purification procedures.

Electronic balance (METTLER TOLEDO INSTRUMENTS CO., LTD.) using EL204 model. The collector constant temperature magnetic stirrer (Henan Yuhua Instrument Co., Ltd.) adopts DF-101S model. Dark box ultraviolet analyzer (Shanghai Jiapeng Technology Co., Ltd.) uses ZF-8 model. Gly/Bet CQDs was synthesized by HT-50H-316L high-pressure hydrothermal synthesis reactor (Anhui Kezhi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd.). JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) was used to take transmission electron microscope (TEM) images at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded by F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane was characterized by Hitachi S-4800 cold field emission scanning electron microscope(SEM)(Hitachi S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). Zeta (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, UK) was used to analyze the potential of Gly/Bet CQDs. The surface morphology of Gly/Bet CQDs was analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Bruker Dimension Icon, Germany). The elements and crystallinity of Gly/Bet CQDs was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker AXS, USA). UV-6100S UV–visible spectrophotometer (Metash Instrument Co., Ltd., China) and 1.0 cm quartz cuvette were used to record the UV–visible absorption spectra. Raman spectra were recorded by HR Evolution high-resolution Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, France). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained using a 250 Xi XPS and Al K Alpha (hv = 1340 eV) in a standard lens mode (Semmerfeld Technologies, Inc., USA). The pH value was measured by SevenExcellence pH meter (Mettler-Toledo Instrument Co., Ltd.). The functional group types of Gly/Bet CQDs in the frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1 were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker Tensor 27, Germany). The FL920 fluorescence lifetime meter (Edinburgh, UK) was used to record the fluorescence lifetime.

The preparation method of Gly/Bet CQDs refers to Jia et al. [32] Gly and Bet were weighed according to the molar ratio of 3:1 and added to a 100 mL three-port flask, and the rotor was added at the same time. The three-necked flask was placed in a heat-collecting thermostatic magnetic stirrer, and the stirring speed was set to 50 rpm·s−1. The mixture was heated at 90 °C until the two formed a transparent uniform stable liquid, which was Gly/Bet deep eutectic solvents (Gly/Bet DES). 30 mL of absolute ethanol was mixed with 1 g of Gly/Bet DES and stirred well. The mixture was put into a teflon reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The yellow CQDs solution was obtained when the reactor was cooled to room temperature. It was poured into a 50 mL centrifuge tube and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to obtain a yellowish solution of Gly/Bet CQDs.

The preparation method of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane was referred to Ansari et al. [33] A mixture of 0.8 g of CMC powder and 50 mL of deionized water was added to a three-necked flask. The solution was continuously stirred at a constant temperature of 65 °C until the CMC powder was fully dissolved, resulting in a clear, colorless solution. Transparent and viscous liquid. 10 mL–50 mL(10 mL intervals)of the prepared CQDs solution was poured into the CMC solution and the solution was shaken vigorously with an ultrasonic cleaner until it was completely mixed. The mixed liquid of CQDs/CMC was slowly poured into the cell culture dish, and the mixed liquid was dried by a blast dryer until the transparent membrane (Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane) was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of toluene gas by Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selective detection of toluene gas, the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane was placed in a gas buffer bottle. Various gases, including toluene, benzene, methanol, ethanol, ethyl acetate, cyclohexane, and acetone, each at a concentration of 2000 ppm, were introduced separately. The membrane was exposed to each gas environment for 30 s. The fluorescence intensity of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane was measured under exposure to each gas, and the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. These measurements and spectra were used to analyze whether the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane exhibits specificity for toluene gas detection.

To study the sensitivity detection of toluene gas, the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane was positioned within a gas buffer bottle and toluene gas of 200–2400 ppm(200 ppm intervals)were introduced and kept at this concentration of toluene gas for 30 s. The fluorescence intensity of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane with various gases was detected. To analyze whether the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane has high sensitivity for toluene gas detection.

According to Eq. 1 [34], the limit of detection (LOD) of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane for toluene gas was calculated.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology of Gly/Bet CQDs. As can be seen from Fig. 1a, Gly/Bet CQDs is spherical with smooth edges, uniform particle size, and almost no particle agglomeration, indicating that Gly/Bet CQDs have been successfully prepared and have good dispersion. The overall distribution of CQDs particle size can be observed from the histogram of particle size distribution in Fig. 1b. It can be seen that the majority of Gly/Bet CQDs is distributed ranging from of 7–10 nm, and the average particle size is 8.37 nm.

The AFM images of the Gly/Bet CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 , which shows the 2D morphology, cross-section profile and 3D morphology of the composite film from left to right, respectively. It can be visualized from the figure that the Gly/Bet CQDs is highly concentrated and evenly distributed, which accords with the results of the TEM (Fig. 1), proving that the Gly/Bet CQDs was successfully synthesized. Fig.S1 is the Zeta diagram of Gly/Bet CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis and fluorescence of Gly/Bet CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of Gly/Bet CQDs was characterized (Fig. 3 ), and the main chemical structures on the surface of Gly/Bet CQDs, as well as the optimal excitation wavelength, maximum emission wavelength and photoluminescence properties of Gly/Bet CQDs was studied. As shown in the figure, the absorption spectrum displays distinct peaks at 210 nm and 280 nm. The prominent peak at 210 nm is attributed to the n → σ* transition associated with the terminal -OH group, while the peak at 280 nm corresponds to the n → π* transition of the CO group. The results showed that Gly/Bet CQDs showed the existence of numerous -OH and CO groups. Fig.S3 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Gly/Bet CQDs at various excitation wavelengths, ranging from 320 to 420 nm with 10 nm intervals, and an EXSlit and EMSlit of 5, respectively. As illustrated in the figure, the fluorescence intensity initially rises and then declines as the excitation wavelength increases. The maximum fluorescence intensity is observed at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and an emission wavelength of 450 nm, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed, reaching 7392 a.u., so 370 nm was the optimal excitation wavelength for Gly/Bet CQDs. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs gradually redshifted, and the highest emission peak was at 420 nm. Results showed that surface-trapped electrons or excitons in Gly/Bet CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior owing to various particle sizes, trapping, and radiative recombination with oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon-containing functional groups. The final prepared Gly/Bet CQDs showed yellow under white light and converted into strong blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig.S2 illustration). As shown in Fig. 1, the fluorescence emission spectrum of Gly/Bet CQD is 370 nm (black curve), and the highest peak is the corresponding emission wavelength, i.e., the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Gly/Bet CQDs measured at 450 nm (red curve). As a result, the excitation wavelength used for subsequent correlated fluorescence spectroscopy analysis was 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence decay curve of Gly/Bet CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
When the FQY is higher, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dot is larger, indicating that the carbon quantum dot has better fluorescence performance and is more suitable as a fluorescent probe for the identification of a substance. The FQY of Gly/Bet CQDs was 16.74 % from Eq. 2 [35] with quinine sulfate as the fluorescence control at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence attenuation curve of Gly/Bet CQDs, which conforms to the double exponential attenuation according to the second-order fitting data, A1 = 0.6253, A2 = 0.3719, τ 1 = 1.833 ns, τ 2 = 5.0914 ns. On average, the lifetime of Gly/Bet CQDs is 4.5 ns (Eq. 3–5 [36–38]), these findings suggest that Gly/Bet CQDs possess a nanosecond fluorescent lifetime devoid of afterglow or phosphorescence. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime gradually decreases with the redshift of the wavelength, indicating that the carbon core area increases. Based on the above analysis, it was found that the photoluminescence of the synthesized CQDs was mainly composed of carbon nucleus light and surface light. As the reaction solvent changes, the degree of carbonization of the precursor changes, resulting in a change in the emission wavelength. The data reveal that Gly/Bet CQDs has heterogeneous fluorescence emission centers, which correspond to the UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of Gly/Bet CQDs. That is, the two fluorescence emission centers correspond to the two absorption peaks in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. When the excitation wavelength increases, the fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs also changes, and the fluorescence emission wavelength is continuously redshifted, that is, Gly/Bet CQDs shows multiple fluorescence emission centers, which conforms to the second-order fitting, which proves that the luminescence effect of Gly/Bet CQDs originates from the energy potential well caused by surface defects. Thus, in the context of functional groups on the surface of Gly/Bet CQDs can effectively detect toluene gas, which can cause changes in fluorescence intensity through specific recognition.

Fig. 5 shows the XPS plot of Gly/Bet CQD, and the raw data is Gaussian fitting to separate the hidden peaks according to the characteristic peak distribution. Three characteristic peaks were clearly displayed in the XPS spectrum: O1s (530 eV), N1s (401 eV) and C1s (284 eV), indicating that Gly/Bet CQDs contained O, N and C elements, and the relative contents were 76.01 %, 5.83 % and 18.17 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C, O, and N were further examined in panels b, c, and d, respectively(Fig. 4). The characteristic peaks of the high-resolution spectrum of C1s were 286.8 eV (O-C=O), 284.8 eV (C=C/C-C), and 283.3 eV (C=O), respectively. The characteristic peaks of the high-resolution spectrum of O1s were 533.5 eV (NO), 532.3 eV (C=O) and 531.5 eV (-OH), respectively, which was mainly owing to the functionalization of oxygen-containing functional groups in the preparation of Gly/Bet CQDs. The characteristic peaks of the high-resolution spectra of N1s were 401.2 eV (NC3), 400.8 eV (-NH+-), 400 eV (C-N-C), and 397.9 eV(-NH-), respectively, indicating that nitrogen was successfully incorporated into Gly/Bet CQDs and formed chemical bonds with carbon. Therefore, XPS studies have shown that the surface of Gly/Bet CQDs is rich in functional groups, indicating that one-step hydrothermal method is an effective method.

Fig. 6 shows the FTIR plot of Gly/Bet CQDs, the Gly/Bet CQDs showed multiple characteristic peaks, among which the wide absorption band at 3350 cm−1 was due to the expansion vibration of N-H/O-H, and the absorption peak at 1328 cm−1 was due to the expansion vibration of the CN bond, indicating that the nitrogen doping was successful. The characteristic peaks at 2933 cm−1, 2837 cm−1, 1633 cm−1 and 1037 cm−1 were related to the expansion and contraction vibrations of CH2, CH, CO and CO, respectively, indicating that there were abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of Gly/Bet CQDs. Overall, Gly/Bet CQDs shows strong tensile vibrations of OH and NH, indicating that they both possess considerable quantities of hydroxyl and amino groups. Therefore, the types of surface functional groups of Gly/Bet CQDs measured by FTIR spectroscopy were basically consistent with the results of XPS determination. Fig.S3 shows the XRD plots of the Gly/Bet CQDs. Raman scattering spectra were used to better analyze the structure of carbon nuclei in CQDs, as demonstrated in Fig.S4. The investigation of fluorescence stability of Gly/Bet CQDs under different pH (Fig.S5), temperature (Fig.S6), and storage time (Fig.S7) all proved it a promising reliable material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different methods for CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The present method was compared with those reported for CQDs (Table 1 ). For the preparation of CQDs, although the size of the carbon quantum dots prepared by these methods is small, there are some problems, such as complex experimental operation, high instrument requirements, and uneven carbon quantum dots. The Gly, Bet and CMC used in this paper are environmentally friendly and simple, and the carbon quantum dots prepared are small in size and uniform in size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The physicochemical properties of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows SEM images of CMC membrane and Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane. Scanning electron microscope photographs of CMC membrane and Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membranes are shown in Fig. 7a and Fig. 7b, respectively. The surface of the pure CMC membrane is smooth and free of impurities. After the addition of Gly/Bet CQDs to the CMC membrane, the surface of the membrane becomes rough and a large number of evenly distributed bright spots appear, which is Gly/Bet CQDs. Gly/Bet CQDs exhibit relatively rough surface topography, which may be due to the lattice structure support of the CMC membrane, which reduces the excessive aggregation of Gly/Bet CQDs and prevents the transfer of non-radiative energy, providing a more efficient method for the radiative transition of the surface state. It is clear that Gly/Bet CQDs are successfully loaded on the CMC membrane and distributed evenly. The presence of Gly/Bet CQDs appears to enhance the homogeneity of the CMC membrane matrix, leading to CQDs are more evenly distributed and improving the integrity of the overall membrane. This is mainly due to the interfacial polymerization reaction between CQDs and CMC membrane. Gly/Bet CQDs act as stabilizers to prevent aggregation and promote better interfacial interactions. This results in a more compact and homogeneous structure. The small size and high surface area of Gly/Bet CQDs contributes to uniform distribution, resulting in improved mechanical integrity and overall structural characteristics of the membrane. This unique structure is conducive to the diffusion of gas molecules on the surface of the membrane, which not only improves the specific surface area of the gas sensitive layer, but also provides more sites for the adsorption of gas molecules, which helps to improve the gas sensing performance. The excellent dispersion of Gly/Bet CQDs on CMC membrane is an important basis for Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane for toluene gas detection.

Fig. 8 shows the EDS and map of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane, Table 2 shows the percentage of elements in Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane. The EDS spectra of Gly/Bet CQDs confirmed the presence of the elements and quantitative testing revealed the amount of each element, which were: C (41 %), N (13.91 %), O (42.64 %), and N (18.26 %), the proportion of O atoms decreased compared with XPS, indicating that Gly/Bet CQDs was successfully adsorbed by the CMC membrane, and it can be seen that the nitrogen has been successfully doped. The uniform distribution of these elements can be seen in Fig. 8b-e, indicating that Gly/Bet CQDs was successfully loaded onto the CMC membrane and dispersed evenly.

The atomic proportions of the elements in the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane are 41 %, 42.64 %, 13.91 %, and 18.26 %, respectively, as indicated in Table 2. The C element concentration rose from 74.95 wt% to 171.30 wt%, demonstrating that Gly/Bet CQDs was loaded and evenly distributed over the CMC membrane.

As a solid matrix, 50 mL of CMC membrane is mixed with 10 mL–50 mL(10 mL intervals) of Gly/Bet, respectively, as shown in Fig.S6. To achieve the solid-state luminescence of Gly/Bet CQDs, 30 mL of the particles were evenly distributed in 50 mL of CMC membrane, which was used as a dispersant. The fluorescence impact of Gly/Bet CQDs in CMC membrane was seen at excitation wavelengths between 310 and 400 nm, with EMSlit of 2.5 and EXSlit of 5 (Fig. 9 ). The emission wavelength of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is 440 nm, as shown in Fig. 9. The interaction between the CQDs and the carrier membrane may alter the defect potential well of the CQDs surface state, causing a slight blue shift compared to the emission wavelength of 450 nm of Gly/Bet CQDs. The Gly/Bet CQDs CQDs@CMC membrane exhibits a clear photoluminescence effect. There is a clear excitation wavelength relationship in the fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs CQDs@CMC membrane, which first rises and then falls as the excitation wavelength increases.

Fig. 10 shows the FTIR plot of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane, it displays the infrared spectral lines of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane (red line) and the CMC membrane (black line). The stretching vibration of N-H/O-H is responsible for the large absorption peak at 3278 cm−1, which is 2915 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1, respectively. Infrared light is absorbed by CH2, C=C/C-H, CO, CN, and C-O-C, respectively, resulting in the stretching vibration peaks at 1612 cm−1, 1319 cm−1, and 1033 cm−1. The diagram shows that the characteristic peak of the functional group in the CMC membrane has a slight red shift after compounding with Gly/Bet CQDs. This means that the wavelength of the maximum absorption peak of the functional group in the composite membrane shifts in the direction of the long wavelength. This shift could be caused by the chromophore (-NH, -OH, etc.) and chromophores in the system by increasing the concentration. After compounding with Gly/Bet CQDs, the functional groups of the CMC membrane are described. As Gly/Bet CQDs are dispersed into the CMC membrane matrix, the infrared absorption intensity of the characteristic peaks increases, indicating the formation of CMC. Hydrogen bonds, then. The electrostatic synergy minimizes Gly/Bet CQDs clustering in the CMC membrane, suppressing concurrent fluorescence aggregation.

XPS tests were performed on Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane to analyze the combined chemical state structure of the surface elemental components of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane. Depending on the distribution of the characteristic peaks, the raw data were Gaussian fitted to isolate the concealed peaks (Fig. 11 ). Three characteristic peaks, O1s (530 eV), N1s (401 eV), and C1s (284 eV), were clearly shown in the XPS full spectrum, indicating that the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane contained the elements of O, N, and C, and the relative contents were 18.50 %, 18.14 %, 64.75 %, and 18.50 %, respectively, 1.14 %, and 64.75 %, respectively, and the elements obtained from XPS analysis were consistent with those contained in Gly/Bet CQDs. The high-resolution spectra of C, O, and N were further analyzed by b, c, and d in Fig. 11. The characteristic peaks of the high-resolution spectra of C1s were 286.5 eV (O-C=O), 284.8 eV (C=C/C-C), and 283.3 eV (C=O); and those of the high-resolution spectra of O1s were 530.3 eV (CO), 534.4 eV (C-O-C), and 534.4 eV (C-O-C), respectively. eV (C-O-C); the high-resolution spectra of N1s are shown to be mainly owed to the absorption vibrations of the C-NH bond. Therefore, the analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy shows that the surface of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane contains abundant functional groups, which proves that the Gly/Bet CQDs was successfully loaded on the CMC membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence decay curve of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Based on Eq. 2, the FQY of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC was calculated at 360 nm as 18.34 %. The fluorescence membrane decay curve of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane (Fig. 12 ). The fluorescence decay curve follows the double exponential decay. Using Eq. 3–5, Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane averaged 5.3 ns. In Fig. 3, Gly/Bet CQDs has a fluorescence life of 4.5 ns. The results showed that Gly/Bet CQDs combined with CMC membrane could improve the FQY and fluorescence lifetime of Gly/Bet CQDs, so that Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane had better fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different methods for Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Thyda et al. [44] synthesized CQDs from mango leaves and prepared membrane with ZnO for ultraviolet photoelectric monitoring. Wang et al. [45] synthesized CQDs by microwave method and synthesized membranes with PCN to enhance their photodynamic activity. Koulivand et al. [46] synthesized CDs by inducing reverse transfection with waste solvent and added to PES casting solution to dry and form a membrane, which was used to improve the anti-fouling of the membrane. Although the above studies have successfully loaded CQDs on membranes, the uniformity of CQDs is poor. The CQDs in this study were uniformly dispersed on the CMC membrane and had uniform particle size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane under different VOCs gases
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMCmembrane measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm after being placed in toluene, benzene, xylene, ethyl acetate, methanol, ethanol, cyclohexane, methane and acetone gas at a concentration of 2000 mg·m−3 for 30 s. Fig. 13b shows the fluorescence enhancement (FF0)/F0 of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane under various VOC gases. (where F0 is the fluorescence intensity measured by Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane at λ ex = 360 nm; F is the fluorescence intensity of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane measured under various VOCs gases). As can be seen from Fig. 13a, the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane fluorescence is slightly quenched in xylene, methanol, ethanol, cyclohexane, methane, and acetone gas environments; The fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane under benzene and ethyl acetate gas increased slightly, and the fluorescence intensity increased by 5.47 %. When the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane was situated in 2000 mg·m−3 toluene gas environment, the fluorescence intensity changed markedly, and the fluorescence intensity increased by 33.2 %. It may be that the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane can increase the contact area of the gas at a concentration of 2000 mg·m−3 toluene. Surface-modified amino CQDs are very sensitive to toluene and have a high efficiency of interaction with toluene, so they have the best response to toluene, resulting in enhanced fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 13b, the (FF0)/F0 value of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is 0.33807 in the toluene gas environment, and the (FF0)/F0 value changes most obviously after the introduction of toluene gas compared to the fluorescence intensity of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane. Therefore, Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC has the best selectivity for toluene gas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence emission spectra of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC methyl bromide at different concentrations of toluene gas
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14a shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane in the environment of toluene gas at concentrations ranging from 200 to 2200 mg·m−3 (with an interval of 200 mg·m−3). It can be seen from the figure that the peak fluorescence intensity of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane gradually increases with the increase of toluene gas concentration, which indicates that the refractive index of the gas in the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane becomes different with the increase of toluene gas concentration, which effectively alters the propagation path of the light in the membrane. The fluorescence intensity of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane did not change significantly when the toluene concentration reached 1600 mg·m−3. The fluorescence enhancement change rate (FF0)/F0 was plotted according to the fluorescence enhancement change rate (FF0)/F0 (Fig. 14b), and the fluorescence intensity of toluene gas concentration was linearly related to the fluorescence intensity of the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane when the concentration of toluene gas ranged from 200 to 1400 mg·m−3. As shown by the fitted straight line in the inset in Fig. 14b, the fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane was linearly related to the toluene gas concentration when the toluene gas concentration was in the range of 200–1400 mg·m−3. And it conforms to the fitted eq. (FF0)/F0 = 4.089× + 0.15634, R2 = 0.9920. According to Eq. 1, the limit of detection (LOD) for toluene gas for the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is 0.452 ppm, and the standard deviation (δ) of the blank sample is 0.61592.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different methods for toluene detection
ce:section.ce:para: The present method was compared with those reported for toluene detection (Table 3 ). For toluene gas, these gas sensors typically have a limited detection range, expensive detecting components, and a challenging setup procedure. With the benefits of low cost and high sensitivity, this paper prepares a CQDs composite membrane for the detection of toluene gas using green, inexpensive propylene Gly, Bet, and CMC as the raw materials. It also offers solid theoretical support for the detection of toluene gas in real-world production settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition mechanism diagram of toluene by Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication method of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane and the mechanism of its detection of toluene (Fig. 15 ). The Gly/Bet CQDs prepared in this experiment contained long carbon chains such as hydrophobic functional groups O-C=O, -NH-、-NO2, which indicated that Gly/Bet CQDs were hydrophobic. Gly/Bet CQDs were combined with CMC membrane to hydrophobically modified the membranes through novel chemical bonding, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic synergies to enable hydrophobic contact between the hydrophobic functional groups of Gly/Bet CQDs and toluene. When the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is exposed to toluene gas, the fluorescence intensity of the membrane increases significantly. Mainly because of the conjugation effect between CO and toluene in Gly/Bet CQDs and the charge transfer that takes place. In the reaction, CO on Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is the electron donor, and -CH3 on toluene is the electron acceptor. They can form a kind of conjugated group C=O-C through hydrogen bond. The conjugated structure is the chromophore group, so the fluorescence intensity increases dramatically when the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane is exposed to toluene gas. The results indicates that Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane has the advantage of high specificity and sensitivity for the detection of toluene gas.

In this study, Gly/Bet CQDs was prepared by a one-step solvothermal method using Gly/Bet DES as the precursor, and the CMC solution was mixed with Gly/Bet CQDs with ultrasonic shaking and dried to obtain Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane, which were characterized in a series of ways. The results showed that Gly/Bet CQDs was uniformly loaded onto CMC membrane. The fluorescence lifetime and FQY were increased to 5.3 ns and 18.34 %, respectively. Gly/Bet CQDs were associated with a slight blue shift compared to Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane. According to the fluorescence quenching/enhancement mechanism of CQDs against toluene, the interaction mechanism between CQDs and the CMC membrane was investigated, the identification mechanism of toluene gas in CMC membrane was also discussed. Fluorescence enhancement rate of change (FF0)/F0. When the toluene gas concentration is 200–1400 ppm, the toluene gas concentration is linearly related to the fluorescence intensity of Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane. The fitting eq. (FF0)/F0 = 4.089× + 0.15634, R2 = 0.9920, and the detection limit LOD was 0.452 ppm. Therefore, the Gly/Bet CQDs@CMC membrane has a strong specificity for the detection of toluene gas, sensitivity and good selectivity, which can well realize the detection of toluene gas, and provides a reliable theoretical basis for the subsequent application of toluene in actual industrial emissions and the detection of toluene gas in life. However, there are still limitations of the fluorescence detection method by membrane. In the actual complex environment, the temperature, humidity, dust in the air and other factors will affect the performance of the membrane. Thus, the detection accuracy of the results may be interfered. Meanwhile, under high temperature, and the aging of membrane under the ultraviolet light from the sun is inevitable, or even accelerated. The limited servicing life needs further study.
